subject: ap-government
theme: The Founders designed a constitutional system rooted in Enlightenment principles — popular sovereignty, limited government, and separation of powers — that balances competing demands for energy in government and protection of individual liberty.

core_concepts.title: Locke's Social Contract & the Declaration
core_concepts.detail: The Declaration of Independence, drawing on Locke's social contract theory, established that legitimate government derives from the consent of the governed and may be altered or abolished when it violates natural rights.

core_concepts.title: Weaknesses of the Articles of Confederation
core_concepts.detail: The Articles of Confederation revealed the dangers of a weak central government — inability to tax, regulate commerce, or enforce laws — and directly motivated the Constitutional Convention of 1787.

core_concepts.title: Federalism & the Supremacy Clause
core_concepts.detail: The Constitution resolves the tension between national power and state sovereignty through federalism: enumerated powers define Congress's authority, the Necessary and Proper Clause extends it, and the Supremacy Clause ensures federal law prevails over state law in conflicts.

core_concepts.title: Federalist Nos. 10 & 51
core_concepts.detail: Federalist No. 10 (Madison) argues that a large republic with many competing factions prevents any single group from becoming a tyrannical majority — the pluralist solution to faction; Federalist No. 51 argues that separation of powers and checks and balances make each branch a check on the others.

core_concepts.title: Brutus No. 1 & the Bill of Rights
core_concepts.detail: Brutus No. 1 (Anti-Federalist) warned that a strong central government with broad taxing power and the Necessary and Proper Clause would eventually destroy state sovereignty and threaten individual liberties — the core argument for a Bill of Rights.

core_concepts.title: The Great Compromise
core_concepts.detail: The Great Compromise (Connecticut Compromise) resolved the small-state vs. large-state dispute by creating a bicameral legislature: proportional representation in the House based on population, equal representation in the Senate with two seats per state.

core_concepts.title: Categorical & Block Grants
core_concepts.detail: Federalism structures intergovernmental relations through grants-in-aid: categorical grants fund specific purposes with strings attached; block grants give states broad discretion; unfunded mandates impose requirements without funding.

exam_tip: The AP exam frequently tests the connections between the Federalist Papers and specific constitutional provisions — know which Federalist number corresponds to which argument (No. 10 = factions/large republic; No. 51 = separation of powers/checks and balances; No. 78 = judicial independence), and always pair Brutus No. 1 with the Anti-Federalist concern that the Necessary and Proper Clause would allow unlimited federal expansion.
